bull_thesis: Tecnicas Reunidas is a balance-sheet turnaround that is already done, but the market is still pricing it as a broken contractor. On top of that, it has an unusually dense calendar of hard-dated events landing inside 60 days. The financials confirm the recovery: revenue grew from 2.81B in 2021 to 6.47B in 2025; net income swung from -190M to +157M; earnings per share went from -3.25 to +2.01 (up 74.8% year over year); backlog (work already contracted but not yet delivered) rose from roughly 11B to about 15B; equity has been restored above its pre-COVID level; and, decisively, the SEPI government rescue loan was repaid early on December 1, 2025, which clears the way to restart the dividend in 2026 (management's words: a 'return to the shareholder remuneration policy'). The company holds genuine net cash (more cash than debt) of -360M on the live read, so there is no debt wall to worry about. Management has a proven habit of under-promising: the 2025 guidance was beaten by 57M ('57M above our initial goal'), and they openly hold back ('if we see contingencies to be released, we would prefer to retain them until the very end'). The transformation program (called SALTA) is producing its first hard proof: services awards of 333M at 25-30% margins, versus roughly 4% for the core engineering, procurement and construction (EPC) work, plus 23 new clients and 11 frame agreements - the highest-margin part of the business is real and scaling toward its 500M target for 2028. Three more structural growth legs are taking shape: Power (ambition raised from about 300M a year to more than 1B a year on demand for AI and data-center electrification), digitalization ('Reimagine TR', an internal estimate of 200M a year in cost savings), and a value-unlocking TR Power spin-off ('completed before summer', with fully separated accounts and the door open to outside capital - a textbook sum-of-the-parts move, valuing each business line on its own). The cluster of upcoming events is exceptional for a company with 6B in revenue: (1) 4-8B of new awards 'most likely before June 13', with 'a very high chance of being awarded around 6 billion'; (2) a Teesside/KPP arbitration ruling 'maybe July'; (3) the TR Power spin-off before summer; and (4) the first dividend. First-quarter 2026 underlying operating profit of 76M (up 36% year over year) already tracks the company's guidance of more than 5% operating margin for 2026, before a one-off 45M war provision. The conditions that have to hold: the Strait of Hormuz reopens without renewed fighting (which would confirm the 45M is a one-time charge, not a recurring floor), the roughly 6B award tranche lands on schedule, and services and power keep improving the mix. If those hold, a de-risked, dividend-paying, backlog-rich contractor with a spun-off power growth engine re-rates from its crisis-discounted level of about 18 times price to free cash flow.
bear_thesis: This is not deep value. It is a thin-margin, low-return oil-and-gas engineering, procurement and construction (EPC) cyclical trading above fair value, with its entire near-term outcome riding on a single macro factor at its most dangerous moment. The screener's own valuation models all agree the stock is expensive, not cheap: discounted cash flow (DCF) fair value is 22.5 against a price of 32.0 (a -42% margin of safety, meaning no cushion at all), and every method is negative (earnings power value -30%, Graham -100%, owner earnings -13%, IV15 -100%). The 'deep value' label is contradicted by the valuation work itself - the survival-discount edge was already captured when the SEPI loan was repaid, so there is no margin of safety left to close. Underlying quality is poor: gross margin is just 4.2% and shrinking, net margin is 2.4%, return on invested capital is 2.2% (below any reasonable cost of capital), the Altman-Z score is 1.6 (in the distress zone despite the net cash), and the Piotroski financial-health score is 5 out of 9. The transformation story is mostly unrealized: services is still only about 254M of 6.47B (roughly 4% of the group), so it cannot move the 4.5% consolidated margin yet, and the claims of more than 1B a year in Power and 200M a year in digitalization savings have zero realized financial footprint. When pressed on turbine slots, analyst Mick Pickup (February 2026) was told 'you can have that turbine in 2030', and management answered with the quality of its relationships rather than secured capacity. The decisive risk is concentration: two-thirds of the backlog sits in the Middle East, directly under a live Iran war and a closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The 45M first-quarter 2026 provision is explicitly conditional ('Strait of Hormuz opening in June, no resume of hostilities') - if fighting resumes, it becomes a floor, not a cap; four projects (about 1.5B of backlog) are already materially disrupted, 1,061 shipments are affected, and reported first-quarter operating profit collapsed from 76M underlying to 31M (latest-quarter EPS down 46% year over year). Cash quality is the chronic weak spot: trailing-twelve-month free cash flow is zero, price to free cash flow is 18.4 times, free cash flow margin is 1.9%, and management deflects every working-capital question with philosophy ('the cash is for my suppliers') rather than a number, refusing even to give a year-end cash target. The chief financial officer's refusal to state the provision recovery assumption ('if I tell you I'm going to recover 60%, I'm telling my clients I'm willing to accept a 40% reduction') signals real downside spread behind that 45M. What would confirm the bear case: a Hormuz re-escalation (the provision becomes a floor, the 2026 guide breaks, free cash flow stays at zero), the 6B award tranche slipping past June 13 or shrinking, or a multi-year litigation or Teesside ruling landing against the company. This is a leveraged, single-factor oil bet wearing a turnaround costume - and the model is already telling you that you are overpaying.
consensus_delta: For a deep-value setup the question is whether the market is pricing terminal decline into a company with stable or growing earnings power - and here it is the opposite. The screener's own models price Tecnicas Reunidas at a premium to intrinsic value (DCF 22.5 versus 32.0, a -42% margin of safety; earnings power value -30%; every method negative). There is no terminal-decline mispricing to exploit; the survival-discount that once made this a value play was already closed when the SEPI loan was repaid early (December 2025) and the dividend was announced. The genuine, narrow gap with the market is about timing and a binary outcome, not valuation: the market is plausibly treating the 45M war provision as the full cost and assuming a clean Hormuz reopening, while management itself flags that the number is a negotiation-anchored estimate ('if I tell you I'm going to recover 60%...') with real spread behind it. The market is also likely modeling the 4-8B award tranche as base-case backlog when it is genuinely binary and dated to 'before June 13'. So the place where we disagree with the market is thin and two-sided: any edge is in correctly handicapping an oil and geopolitical binary that the market cannot price precisely, not in a fundamental value gap. Under the current backdrop (a complacent melt-up sitting on an unresolved oil and Hormuz shock), the market's willingness to re-rate an oil-EPC name on awards is capped until the macro tail resolves - the market is not looking the wrong way, it is correctly waiting for Hormuz.
forcing_function: The dated catalysts - the events that force the re-rating - are unusually concrete for a company with 6B in revenue, but they straddle the oil tail. In order: (1) around June 13, 2026 - the 4-8B award decision ('most likely before June 13', 'very high chance around 6 billion'); a clean roughly 6B print is the single fastest trigger to re-rate the stock up, and a miss the fastest to re-rate it down. (2) End of June 2026 - the Strait of Hormuz reopening that the 45M provision is underwritten on; a benign resolution validates the one-off charge and the 2026 guide, while re-escalation breaks both. (3) Around July 2026 - the Teesside/KPP arbitration ruling ('final ruling from the arbitrator maybe July'), a multi-year contingent-liability overhang that resolves to either a clean release or a cash hit. (4) Before summer 2026 - completion of the TR Power spin-off, the sum-of-the-parts event that crystallizes value (with optional outside capital into the power unit). (5) The first 2026 dividend declaration. The cluster is dense and near at hand; the catch is that catalysts (1) through (3) all share the same root variable - oil and Hormuz - so they are not independent drivers but four expressions of one bet.
valley_of_death: The risky window - the months just ahead - holds a live macro tail rather than a financing wall. There is no debt-maturity problem (net cash of -360M, SEPI repaid), so the danger is operational and outside the company's control: (1) A Hormuz re-escalation - the 45M provision is explicitly conditioned on 'Strait of Hormuz opening in June, no resume of hostilities'; if fighting resumes, that 45M flips from a cap to a floor, the four disrupted projects (about 1.5B of backlog, 1,061 shipments affected) bleed further, and the 2026 guide of more than 5% operating margin breaks. The current macro read names exactly this tripwire ('Oil/Hormuz re-escalation: WTI breaks higher, VIX re-spikes toward 26'), and WTI crude oil is already around 92 versus a 200-day moving average of 75. (2) Cash conversion - trailing-twelve-month free cash flow is zero and the business depends structurally on down-payments; if the 6B award tranche slips, the down-payment inflow that funds working capital and the dividend is delayed, and management has refused to give any year-end cash target. (3) The award binary itself - 4-8B 'most likely before June 13'; a miss or a shrink to the low end is a near-term de-rating event. (4) Latest-quarter EPS is already down 46% year over year on the provision, so reported first-half 2026 optics are impaired regardless. The risky window is short and sharp and centered on a single oil and geopolitical variable the macro backdrop explicitly tells us to penalize.
positioning_washout: This is the inverse of a classic dividend-cut flush - the shareholder base is in the middle of a constructive rotation that creates a different, more subtle risk of forced selling. Capital allocation has just pivoted from debt repair ('all the cash is for my suppliers', 2024-25) to shareholder returns ('return to the shareholder remuneration policy... dividend payments resuming', February 2026), which is beginning to attract a fresh, yield-focused base onto a stock whose underlying cash flow (trailing-twelve-month free cash flow of zero) does not yet robustly cover a growing payout. The mechanical risk is therefore forward-looking: if the Hormuz disruption suppresses cash conversion and the first dividend is trimmed or deferred, the newly arrived yield holders sell out of a name that momentum and quality funds also avoid (Altman-Z 1.6, return on invested capital 2.2%). Separately, the TR Power spin-off ('before summer') is a deliberate forced-realization event: holders of Tecnicas Reunidas who do not want the standalone power stub will sell the spun shares - a clean technical overhang on that stub, and a familiar special-situations pattern. Net: no immediate forced-selling flush in the parent today, but two engineered washout vectors (a dividend that may disappoint a brand-new yield base, and a spin-off distribution) are loaded for the second half of 2026.
moderator_conclusion: The call is to wait for the wash-out: put this on the watchlist, conviction 2 - the right entry is after the oil binary clears, not before. The franchise is genuinely strengthening (revenue 2.8B to 6.5B, net income -190M to +157M, SEPI repaid early, real net cash, services proving out at 25-30% margins, a first dividend, and a value-unlocking TR Power spin-off), and the dated catalyst calendar (June 13 awards, July Teesside, pre-summer spin) is exactly the kind of hard-dated, in-window cluster the current backdrop rewards. On those two factors alone this could argue for an aggressive buy-now at high conviction. But the macro read moves the call down, decisively, on two of its own explicit rules. First, the catalysts are not independent: the backdrop warns hardest against theses that hinge on one shared macro factor (like oil or AI-capex), and Tecnicas Reunidas is the textbook violation - two-thirds of backlog in the Middle East, the entire bull case riding on Hormuz reopening, and three of its four catalysts (the 6B awards, the provision validation, even the Teesside timing) all rooted in the same oil and geopolitical variable. This is an 'oil-shock-resolves' rescue bet, structurally identical to the Fed-cut rescue theses the backdrop tells us to penalize, and it sits directly on the live 'Oil/Hormuz re-escalation' tripwire (WTI crude already around 92 versus a 200-day moving average of 75, the April VIX-to-26 precedent still fresh). Second, margin of safety: this is tagged deep value but the screener's own models unanimously say it is roughly 30-42% expensive (DCF 22.5 versus 32.0; earnings power value, Graham, owner earnings and IV15 all negative) - the survival-discount edge was already captured at the SEPI repayment, so there is no value cushion to absorb a bad oil outcome. The one tailwind in the backdrop - the TR Power spin-off lands in the favored structural-special-situations lane (spinoffs rank high) - is real but secondary to a core that is an oil-EPC cyclical, not a clean special situation, and it does not offset zero trailing-twelve-month free cash flow and an Altman-Z of 1.6. Net: the dated catalysts keep this off an outright pass (it is not a value trap and not dead money - a benign Hormuz print plus a roughly 6B award could re-rate it sharply), but the single-factor oil dependence the backdrop explicitly penalizes, combined with no margin of safety, caps conviction at 2 and the verdict at wait-for-the-wash-out. The specific event to wait for: confirmation that the Strait of Hormuz has reopened with no resumed hostilities and the June 13 award tranche prints at or above roughly 6B - that pair simultaneously de-risks the macro tail and the binary, at which point the dividend and spin-off re-rating can be underwritten with independence restored. Entering before that is paying a premium price to be long one oil variable into a complacent tape over a live tail.
interrogator_dossier:
# A deep dive on management and the setup - Tecnicas Reunidas (TRE.MC) - Industrials / Engineering, Procurement and Construction (EPC)

*Eight consecutive quarterly calls, August 3, 2024 through May 14, 2026, cross-referenced against the screener's hard financial trajectory.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story over these eight quarters is one of a **post-COVID balance-sheet repair that succeeded, wrapped in a strategic-transformation narrative ('SALTA') that is still mostly a promise - and which, on the very last call, collided with a live geopolitical shock.**

**Act I - 'We will double margins to 8% by 2028' (August 2024).** The opening call is pure strategy theater. Chairman Juan Lladó walks through the SALTA strategy's 'seven pillars' (services, North America, decarbonization, EPC-with-partners, digitalization, talent) unveiled at the May 2024 Abu Dhabi Capital Markets Day, anchored on one number: *'doubling to 8% ... 2028 operating margins.'* The thesis is a re-rating from a low-margin, balance-sheet-impaired EPC contractor into a higher-margin, services-led, de-risked engineering partner. Order intake is light (1.4B euros in the first half) but management insists *'it wouldn't be a big challenge to replace 2024 sales.'*

**Act II - 'The backlog is record and healthy' (November 2024 to May 2025).** The emphasis shifts from strategy slides to **commercial proof points**: a record 12.4B euro backlog (November 2024), then 12.5B euros (full-year 2024), then a step-change to 14.9B euros (first quarter 2025) on the 7.3B euro Lower Zakum award. The recurring refrain becomes *'healthy'* backlog 'with a strategic partner' (almost always Sinopec). The de-risking sub-narrative - partnering on EPC to offload execution risk - is consistent and credible.

**Act III - 'Services is breaking the glass ceiling' (August 2025 to February 2026).** The transformation narrative gains its first hard evidence. Services awards reach 333M euros for full-year 2025 at *'25% to 30%'* margins (versus about 4% for group EPC), with 23 new clients and 11 frame agreements. Two new growth legs are *introduced*: (i) **Power** - ambition raised from about 300M euros a year historically to *'more than 1 billion euros per year'*, justified by AI and data-center electrification demand, culminating in the **TR Power spin-off** ('before summer'); and (ii) **digitalization / 'Reimagine TR'** - a claim that *'around 60% of the man hours in our sector can be automated'* yielding *'200 million euros per year in cost efficiencies.'* Full-year 2025 prints the cleanest slide in years: the SEPI rescue loan **repaid early (December 1, 2025)**, a dividend resumption announced for 2026, and 2026 guidance of more than 6.5B euros in sales, more than 5% operating margin, and about 200M euros of net profit.

**Act IV - The shock (May 2026).** The first-quarter 2026 call is structurally different - management leads with a dedicated **'Middle East conflict'** section. An Iran war has closed the Strait of Hormuz; **two-thirds of backlog is in the Middle East**; four projects 'deep inside construction' (about 1.5B euros of backlog) are materially disrupted; 1,061 shipments are affected; and a **45M euro provision** cuts reported first-quarter operating profit from 76M euros underlying to **31M euros**. At the same time, management dangles the single most concrete catalyst of the entire series: **4-8B euros of awards 'most likely before June 13'** (about 45 days out), with *'a very high chance of being awarded around 6 billion euros.'* The arc thus ends on maximum tension: the structural transformation is finally producing numbers, a near-term award catalyst is hard-dated - but the entire backlog's center of gravity sits directly under the oil-shock blast radius.

**Growth drivers quietly dropped or re-weighted:** Decarbonization and hydrogen, the loud headline pillar of 2024, is steadily demoted - by November 2025 management concedes *'very few projects have been launched this year'* and earlier admitted the *'hype has disappeared'* (November 2024). Steel decarbonization, hyped in August 2024, fades to clients *'moving from hydrogen to carbon capture.'* The growth narrative has rotated from **decarbonization to services to power and AI-electrification** - each pivot chasing the prevailing capital-markets theme.

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

The hard numbers **corroborate the balance-sheet-repair and revenue-growth story, but expose the '8% margin' transformation thesis as still almost entirely unrealized, and flag a profitability base far thinner than the headline EPS suggests.**

| Management claim | Quarter | The financials say | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| 'Doubling to 8% operating margins by 2028' | Aug 2024 (Capital Markets Day) | Full-year 2025 group operating margin **4.5%**; gross margin in screener **4.2% (contracting)**; net margin **2.4%** | **Unproven.** About half-way at best; the entire re-rating thesis lives in the years 2026-2028, which have not happened. |
| Revenue and backlog growth is real | All | Revenue **2021 2.81B euros to 2025 6.47B euros**; up 45.3% year over year in 2025, 3-year compound growth 15.2%; backlog 11B to about 15B euros | **Strongly confirmed.** Top-line growth is the most credible part of the story. |
; EPS and profit inflecting; Full-year 2025; Net income **2021 -190M euros to 2025 +157M euros**; EPS -3.25 to **+2.01**; up 74.8% year over year; **Confirmed directionally**, but off a deeply negative base - this is *recovery*, not secular compounding (EPS 3-year compound growth **+0.0%**).; 
| Services at '25-30%' margin is a margin game-changer | Aug 2025 to Feb 2026 | Services revenue only **254M euros of 6.47B euros (about 4% of group)**; group margin still 4.5% | **True but immaterial so far.** The high-margin mix is real but too small to move the consolidated needle yet. |
; 'Best-ever quarterly operating profit ... 64M euros' / '12th quarter of growth'; Aug 2025; Corroborated by the trajectory; full-year 2025 operating profit 291M euros (up 61%); **Confirmed.**; 
| Net cash / balance sheet healthy, SEPI repaid | Full-year 2025, Q1 2026 | **Net cash -360M euros (live, net cash) / -201M euros (scan)**; SEPI participating loan repaid December 2025 | **Confirmed - the genuine bright spot.** Real net cash, no leverage problem. |
| 2026 guide: more than 6.5B euros sales, more than 5% operating margin, about 200M euros net income | Feb 2026 | Q1 2026 *underlying* operating profit 76M euros (up 36%) tracks the more-than-5% path **before** the 45M euro war provision; **reported** Q1 operating profit 31M euros, latest-quarter EPS year over year **-46%**, **trailing-twelve-month free cash flow 0 euros** | **At risk.** The underlying run-rate supports the guide; the conflict provision and a zero-free-cash-flow trailing year directly threaten the 2026 delivery. |
| Cash converts from EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization); 'discipline with cash' | Recurring | Trailing-twelve-month **free cash flow 0 euros**; free cash flow margin **1.9%**; price to free cash flow **18.4 times** | **The soft underbelly.** Management deflects on working capital every single quarter; free cash flow is structurally weak and lumpy (down-payment-driven), and is currently *zero* on a trailing-twelve-month basis. |

**The two numbers that puncture the bull narrative:** (1) **Return on invested capital 2.2% / gross margin 4.2%** - this is a thin-margin, low-return contractor whose returns on capital do not cover any reasonable cost of capital; the 'transformation' must deliver or the stock is a low-quality cyclical. (2) **DCF fair value 22.5 euros versus price 32.0 euros**, with *every* valuation method showing a negative margin of safety (DCF -42%, earnings power value -30%, Graham -100%, owner earnings -13%, IV15 -100%). On the screener's own models, **Tecnicas Reunidas is not cheap - it is roughly 30-40% expensive**, which is a critical and counterintuitive finding for a 'deep value' tag.

**Where the story is RICHER than the numbers:** the Power (1B euros a year) and digitalization (200M euros a year in savings) claims - both are pipeline and ambition with essentially zero realized financial footprint in the trajectory. **Where it is POORER than the numbers:** management undersells how genuinely repaired the balance sheet is (real net cash, SEPI gone, equity back above pre-COVID) - the survival risk that justified the depressed multiple has largely been retired.

---

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: rising and increasingly specific from August 2024 to February 2026, then sharply defensive in May 2026.** A clear arc from offensive (growth and strategy) to, at the very end, damage control.

- **August 2024 - measured-optimistic, hedged on timing.** *'It wouldn't be a big challenge to replace 2024 sales.'* On decarbonization already cautious: *'things are not moving as fast as probably we expected ... but I would never say we are negative.'* Offensive, but with audible hedges.
- **November 2024 - confident, 'record' language.** *'This is not a challenge anymore ... record backlog of 12.4 billion euros.'* First overt swagger.
- **February 2025 - most confident of the series, leads with guidance.** Breaks house convention: *'Usually, guidance is left to the end ... this presentation will flow much better if we start disclosing our targets.'* Volunteers both 2025 and 2026 ambitions and a **dividend-return** signal. Volunteering forward numbers is a genuine confidence tell.
- **May 2025 / August 2025 - peak operational confidence.** *'12th quarter in a row of growth ... always aligned with our previous guidance'*; *'highest ever quarterly operating profit'*; *'we have broken a glass ceiling'* on services.
- **February 2026 - triumphant.** *'Exceeded all initial expectations ... a real inflection point ... we have managed to place TR where we wanted TR to be.'* SEPI repaid, dividend resuming, spin-off announced.
- **May 2026 - abruptly defensive.** Language pivots to *'we have to be and we are being prudent and realistic'* (repeated), *'a wide-shot provision of 45 million euros.'* The chairman bristles defensively when pushed on rebuild capital spending: *'it's not a real question ... there's not such a big damage.'* The shift from 'inflection point' to 'prudent and realistic' in one quarter is the single most important tonal datapoint in the dossier.

The offensive-to-defensive rotation is **event-driven (an exogenous war), not a slow erosion of the operating story** - an important distinction for the director: the franchise narrative did not crack; an oil shock landed on top of a still-improving business.

---

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: medium-high.** The track record over the window is good and improving, with a consistent, *deliberate* conservatism - but the screener's beat-rate (4 of 7, 57%) and the brand-new war provision keep this out of 'high.'

- **Full-year 2024 guide (4% margin, replace sales) - delivered:** 4.5B euros sales, 4.1% margin, backlog 12.5B euros. **Met.**
- **Full-year 2025 guide (February 2025: more than 5.2B euros sales, about 4.5% margin) - over-delivered massively:** actual **6.5B euros sales (up 45%), 4.5% margin, 291M euros operating profit - '57M above our initial goal.'** A large, clean beat. Management explicitly framed it as exceeding expectations.
- **Sandbagging pattern: yes, and openly admitted.** *'If we see contingencies to be released, we would prefer to retain them until the very end ... we are not going to get crazy. We want to be conservative'* (February 2025). They systematically hold margin contingencies - consistent with the over-delivery and a reason to trust the more-than-5% 2026 underlying guide.
- **The check against the screener:** the earnings beat rate is **4 of 7 (57%)** - the over-delivery is real but not metronomic; they have missed roughly 3 of 7 quarters, and **latest-quarter EPS came in -46% year over year** under the provision. The conservative-guide habit is a credibility asset; the lumpiness and the fresh provision are why it is medium-*high*, not high.

**Net:** when management gives a number with the strait open, believe it slightly under-promised. The 2026 guide (more than 5% operating margin, about 200M euros net income) is credible on the *underlying* run-rate; the binary variable is now exogenous (Hormuz), not managerial.

---

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

Three topics recur where analysts press and management *systematically deflects* - and one of them has now bitten.

1. **Working capital and cash flow - the perennial deflection.** Álvaro Lenze (November 2024, February 2025), Filipe Leite (multiple), and Juan Cánovas (May 2026) all push on *why receivables keep climbing* and *what net cash will actually be.* Management's answer never changes: *'if you have bigger sales ... the immediate consequence is that you have bigger accounts receivable,'* and *'if I have cash, the cash is for my suppliers ... if the cash is for investors, I'm afraid I'm not doing the correct thing.'* The chief financial officer repeatedly **refuses to give a year-end cash target** (*'that's a tricky question'*). This is the clearest evasion in the series and maps directly to the **trailing-twelve-month free cash flow of 0 euros / 18.4 times price to free cash flow** weakness - the one place the financials and the narrative are quietly at odds.
2. **Litigation and disputes (Teesside-KPP Finland, others) - chronic non-answer.** Filipe Leite (November 2024), and again (May 2026), ask for resolution timing; management's stance is fixed: *'I don't think it's correct or sensible to talk about litigations in public,'* softened only to *'we feel comfortable ... a large percentage of our disputes will be very much finished this year'* (May 2025) and finally a concrete *'final ruling from the arbitrator maybe July'* (May 2026). A multi-year overhang that analysts will not let go of.
3. **Turbine supply for the 1B euro Power ambition - unresolved.** Mick Pickup (February 2026): *'if you want a turbine ... you can have that turbine in 2030. So how does TR get in that queue?'* Management answers with the quality of its relationships ('the OEMs call us'), **not** with secured slots - a real gap under the most-hyped new growth leg.
4. **Capacity to execute a step-up in awards - pushed repeatedly, answered confidently.** Juan Cánovas (May 2025) and Ignacio Doménech (May 2026) probe whether TR can absorb 4-8B euros of new awards; here management is *credibly* specific (India headcount up 4x in 3 years, about 13,500 staff, satellite offices) - this one is a strength, not an evasion.

**The tell:** every cash and working-capital question is met with philosophy ('discipline,' 'suppliers first') rather than a number. For a deep-value, where-we-disagree-with-the-market thesis, that is the pressure point most likely to disappoint.

---

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

**Red flags**
- **Two-thirds of backlog in the Middle East, into a live Hormuz and oil shock** (May 2026). The 45M euro provision is explicitly conditioned on *'Strait of Hormuz opening in June, no resume of hostilities'* - if fighting resumes, the provision is a floor, not a cap. Maximum single-factor concentration at the worst moment.
- **Chronic working-capital opacity plus trailing-twelve-month free cash flow of 0 euros** - operating profit grows but cash conversion is repeatedly deflected and is currently nil; the business is structurally down-payment-dependent for cash.
- **Altman-Z 1.6 (distress zone) and return on invested capital 2.2%** - despite the net cash, the composite solvency and return profile remains low-quality cyclical, not a fortress.
- **Every valuation model says expensive** (DCF 22.5 euros versus 32.0 euros; all five methods show a negative margin of safety) - the 'deep value' label is contradicted by the screener's own DCF, earnings power value and Graham.
- **Decarbonization pillar quietly demoted** - a headline 2024 growth driver shrank to *'very few projects launched'*; the narrative rotates to whatever theme is hot (now AI and power).
- **Provision-driven -46% latest-quarter EPS year over year** - the first real earnings dent of the series, and it is exogenous and potentially recurring.

**Green flags**
- **SEPI rescue loan repaid early (December 1, 2025), leading to a dividend resumption in 2026** - the balance-sheet repair is *complete and verified*, retiring the survival-risk discount.
- **Genuine net cash (-360M euros live)**, equity restored above pre-COVID - no leverage wall.
- **Backlog 11B to about 15B euros with strategic partners (Sinopec)** - multi-year revenue visibility, execution risk shared.
- **Services proving out (333M euros of awards, 25-30% margins, 23 new clients)** - the highest-margin leg of the thesis is real, just small.
- **Conservative-guidance habit validated by a 57M euro full-year 2025 over-delivery** - management under-promises.
- **Hard-dated near-term catalysts**: 4-8B euros of awards 'before June 13,' a Teesside arbitration ruling 'maybe July,' a TR Power spin-off 'before summer,' and the first dividend - an unusually dense, dated catalyst cluster.

---

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Management/voice change mid-series.** From November 2025 the chairman is rendered as **'Juan Arburua'** (transcription) leading, and a new investor-relations voice (Antonio Alonso-Muñoyerro) appears, while chief financial officer **Eduardo San Miguel** is the constant. The CFO's 20-year tenure (*'being the CFO of this company for 20 years'*) is the anchor of credibility; his fingerprints are on every cash answer - and every cash deflection.
- **'Healthy' is the tell-word.** Backlog is described as *'healthy'* relentlessly (November 2024 onward), a deliberate contrast to TR's pre-2021 history of toxic fixed-price losses. The *de-risking-via-partners* message (Sinopec on nearly every megaproject) is the real, under-appreciated structural change - TR is converting from a lump-sum-EPC risk-taker into a lower-risk partner and services provider, which is why margins can rise without the old blow-up risk.
- **Spin-off as a value-crystallization signal.** The **TR Power** spin-off (announced February 2026, 'before summer') with *'fully separated financial accounts'* and its own management and logo is a textbook sum-of-the-parts move - management is explicitly trying to get the market to value the 1B-euro-a-year power growth leg separately. On the Q1 2026 call they leave the door open to *external capital* for TR Power (*'it will take a couple of years to fully decouple'*). This is the most activist-friendly, structural catalyst in the file.
- **Capital-allocation pivot.** The narrative shifts from *'all cash goes to suppliers'* (2024-25, debt-repair mode) to *'return to shareholder remuneration ... dividend payments resuming'* (February 2026). A genuine regime change in how the company intends to use cash - and a setup for a new, yield-oriented shareholder base.
- **Hedging on the provision is information.** The CFO's refusal to state the recovery assumption (*'If I tell you ... I'm going to recover 60% ... I'm telling my clients I'm willing to accept a 40% reduction'*) is a tell that the 45M euros is a *negotiation-anchored* estimate, not an engineering certainty - real downside spread sits behind that number.

---

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: strengthening (operationally and on the balance sheet) - temporarily turning on an exogenous oil and Hormuz shock.**

Across eight quarters, Tecnicas Reunidas executed a credible turnaround: revenue 2.8B to 6.5B euros, losses to 157M euros of net profit, the SEPI rescue loan repaid early, equity restored, a first dividend resuming, backlog near 15B euros, and the first hard evidence (services at 25-30% margins) that the SALTA margin-up thesis is more than a slide. Management's conservative-guidance habit is validated by a 57M euro full-year 2025 over-delivery. That is a strengthening franchise.

**But the single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing is concentration risk versus catalyst timing - and they point in opposite directions.** The *entire* thesis now hinges on one shared macro factor: **oil and the Strait of Hormuz, where two-thirds of the backlog sits.** The 45M euro provision is a *conditional* number ('strait open in June, no resumed hostilities'); a re-escalation makes it a floor and can decimate 2026 delivery and a still-zero trailing-twelve-month free cash flow. Against that, an unusually dense, hard-dated catalyst cluster fires inside about 60 days: **4-8B euros of awards 'before June 13,' a Teesside arbitration ruling 'maybe July,' the TR Power spin-off 'before summer,' and the first dividend.** And critically, on the screener's own models the stock is **not cheap (DCF 22.5 euros versus 32.0 euros, every method showing a negative margin of safety)** - the easy survival-discount edge has already been harvested.

The director must therefore decide whether the dated award and spin-off catalysts can re-rate the stock *before* the oil tail either resolves benignly (validating the provision and unlocking 6B euros of awards) or re-escalates (turning the provision into a floor). This is **not a margin-of-safety deep-value setup** - the value gap is gone and the model says expensive - it is a **leveraged bet on a single macro variable wrapped around a real catalyst calendar.** Size for the oil tail, not for the backlog.

Management credibility 4 of 5; trajectory strengthening.
